paragraphs.text: Thank you for the opportunity to give feedback on the fairness of digital transactions and interactions. I find troubling that companies that provide software licenses and services routinely use vague and misleading wording to deceive the consumers into overestimating the value of their offerings. Often the words used are "purchase" or "buy" implying that the consumer buys the actual software in its entirety, while the offer is only for a license to use a software or a service where the providers retains full propriety over the software in question and the customer does not obtain any of that. Another example of misleading behavior can be seen on the duration of the license agreement, which is often hidden within pages and pages of end user agreements that can be read only after purchasing the license itself or not mentioned at all, implying that the license is actually eternal. Despite that the reality is that often these license can be made not usable by the customer whenever the software provider decides to stop supporting the means that the licensee uses to access the product. As a personal example I purchased a copy of the video game Bioshock about a year after its release in 2007. The game was both critically acclaimed and commercially successful. It has been praised for its exploration of philosophical concepts as objectivism. I add this information to clarify that it is a historically important title for the media as a whole. While the game was sold with a required internet authenticity verification no time limit was present. Despite that after a few years the game publisher shut down the authentication server in a cost cutting measure, making me unable to install and play the game on new PC despite not having reached the activation limit. While the publisher offered to exchange the license with one for a digital copy using a different means of authentication it made no effort to reach out to all their customers and time limited this initiative, meaning me and many others that purchased the physical copy of the game like me failed to swap it in time. It would be much fairer if the language used by the software provider describes more clearly their offers, for example using the word "license" or "gain access" instead of "buy" or "purchase" when the software is licensed and not sold. It would also be fairer if the customer is always told explicitly the proposed duration of the license before they purchase it. For video games in particular, this and other preservation issues are tackled by the Stop Killing Games citizen initiative, that has already gathered over 1,4 millions signatures. It might be beneficial to investigate how the initiative could be expanded to all software and not only video games. Recently cases of software and media being unfairly removed happened due to pressure from payment services. While the motivation behind these removals seems agreeable at first sight, being the protection of children and women from the effects media apologetic of violent and degrading behavior against them, the means used to pursue it clearly exemplify why payment processors are not the right actors for this delicate job and should relent its execution to the proper institutions instead. In a cost cutting effort that shows how the only interest of the actors is economic and not humanitarian in nature, the pressure translated in a canvas delisting or deindexing of smaller titles that treated adult and delicate themes, without care whether the themes were treated in a constructive or harmful way. As an example the game "He Fucked the Girl Out of Me" has been deindexed from Itch.io despite receiving several prizes for its treatment of themes like trauma and gender identity, as the DFA DocLab Award for Digital Storytelling and the the jury award at the 2022 Melbourne Queer Games Festival. This is only one of many legal and even socially important games hit by the processor acting without the due diligence or care.
